title: Understanding Mortgages
subtitle: Big Picture: What You’re Really Buying
content:
Today’s Goal

Understand how a mortgage works and what actually determines your monthly payment.

  • What a mortgage is
  • Down payments
  • Interest rates & APR
  • Amortization (why early payments feel unfair)
  • Fixed vs ARM
  • PMI, escrow, closing costs

We’ll use simple numbers and real-life terms you’ll see on lender websites.

title: What Is a Mortgage?
subtitle: Loan + House = Collateral
content:
Definition

A mortgage is a loan used to buy a home.

  • You get the house now
  • The bank gets paid back over time
  • The house is collateral: if you don’t pay, the lender can take it (foreclosure)
Key idea: You’re not just paying for a house… you’re paying for time (interest).

title: Mortgage Vocabulary
subtitle: Principal • Interest • Term
content:
PrincipalThe amount you borrow
InterestThe cost of borrowing (rate %)
TermHow long you have to repay (e.g., 30 years)
Monthly paymentUsually includes principal + interest (+ taxes/insurance if escrow)

Most people quote payment as PITI: Principal, Interest, Taxes, Insurance.

title: Down Payment
subtitle: Why It Matters
content:
Down payment = money you pay upfront

  • Reduces how much you borrow
  • Often improves your interest rate
  • Can eliminate PMI if you put down 20%

$400,000 home

10% down = $40,000 → borrow $360,000

20% down = $80,000 → borrow $320,000

title: Loan-to-Value (LTV)
subtitle: The 20% Threshold
content:
LTV = (Loan ÷ Home Value)

LTV = Loan / Value
  • If LTV is high, lender risk is higher
  • PMI is common when LTV > 80%

$360,000 loan on a $400,000 home

LTV = 360,000 / 400,000 = 90%

title: Interest Rate vs APR
subtitle: Not the Same Number
content:
Interest Rate

The % charged on the loan balance.

APR (Annual Percentage Rate)

Rate plus some fees spread over time. Helps compare loans.

Rule: APR is usually higher than the interest rate.

When comparing lenders, look at both the monthly payment and APR.

title: What Determines Your Payment?
subtitle: The Big 4 Inputs
content:
Your monthly payment depends on:

  1. Loan amount (price − down payment)
  2. Interest rate
  3. Term (15 vs 30 years)
  4. Taxes & insurance (often escrowed)

Changing the interest rate even a little can change the payment a lot.

title: 15-Year vs 30-Year
subtitle: Lower Total Interest vs Higher Payment
content:
Trade-off

  • 15-year: higher monthly payment, much less total interest
  • 30-year: lower monthly payment, much more total interest
You’re paying for time. 30 years = more time = more interest.

title: Amortization
subtitle: Why Early Payments Feel Like ‘All Interest’
content:
Amortization schedule

A table showing each monthly payment and how it splits into:

  • Interest (cost of borrowing)
  • Principal (paying down the loan)
Early on: interest is a larger part of the payment.

title: Amortization Example
subtitle: One Payment Split
content:
Suppose your monthly payment (P&I) is $2,200.

Month 1 might be:

  • Interest: $1,900
  • Principal: $300

Later, it flips: more principal, less interest.

You’re not being ‘scammed’—it’s the math of how interest is calculated on the remaining balance.

title: Why Rates Matter So Much
subtitle: Small % Change → Big $ Change
content:
Rates are powerful

On a large loan, even a 1% change is huge over 30 years.

Higher rate = higher payment + much higher total interest.

This is why people care so much about timing the market and refinancing.

title: Fixed-Rate Mortgage
subtitle: Stable Payment (for P&I)
content:
Fixed-rate

  • Interest rate stays the same for the entire term
  • Principal + Interest payment stays the same

Taxes/insurance can still change over time, so the total payment (PITI) can change.

title: Adjustable-Rate Mortgage (ARM)
subtitle: Lower Start, More Risk
content:
ARM basics

  • Rate changes after an initial fixed period (e.g., 5/1 ARM)
  • Payment can increase if rates rise
Question to ask: Can you afford the payment if it adjusts upward?

title: Points & Rate Buydowns
subtitle: Pay Upfront to Lower the Rate
content:
Mortgage points

  • 1 point = 1% of the loan amount (paid upfront)
  • Can reduce your interest rate

$360,000 loan

1 point = $3,600 upfront

Worth it depends on how long you keep the loan (break-even).

title: Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI)
subtitle: The ‘Less Than 20% Down’ Fee
content:
PMI

An extra monthly cost that protects the lender if you default.

  • Common when down payment < 20%
  • Can often be removed once you reach ~80% LTV
PMI does not protect you. It protects the lender.

title: Escrow
subtitle: Taxes & Insurance Collected Monthly
content:
Escrow account

The lender collects money monthly for:

  • Property taxes
  • Homeowners insurance

Then pays those bills when due.

Escrow can make your payment higher than principal+interest alone.

title: Closing Costs
subtitle: Fees Beyond the Down Payment
content:
Common closing costs

  • Loan origination fees
  • Appraisal
  • Title insurance
  • Attorney fees (varies by state)
  • Prepaid taxes/insurance
Typical range: ~2%–5% of purchase price (varies widely).

title: Affordability
subtitle: DTI and the Real Budget
content:
Debt-to-Income (DTI)

Lenders look at your monthly debts vs income.

  • Lower DTI = easier approval
  • High DTI = riskier borrower

Being approved doesn’t mean it’s comfortable. Always stress-test your budget.

title: Credit Score & Rate
subtitle: Why Credit Impacts the Cost
content:
Credit score affects:

  • Whether you’re approved
  • Your interest rate
  • Sometimes PMI cost
Better credit can save you tens of thousands over the life of a loan.

title: Refinancing
subtitle: Resetting the Loan
content:
Refinance = replace your mortgage

  • Goal: lower rate, lower payment, or change term
  • But it comes with closing costs again
Key question: Will the monthly savings exceed the refi costs before you move?

title: Common Pitfalls
subtitle: How People Get Burned
content:
Watch out for:

  • Focusing only on the monthly payment
  • Ignoring taxes/insurance changes
  • Taking an ARM without understanding adjustments
  • Underestimating maintenance costs
  • Overborrowing because you ‘got approved’

title: Quick Case Study
subtitle: $400k Home, 10% Down
content:
Home price: $400,000

Down payment (10%): $40,000

Loan: $360,000

Then add:

  • Principal + Interest (depends on rate/term)
  • Taxes
  • Insurance
  • PMI (likely, because <20% down)

This is why two people with the same home price can have very different payments.

title: What To Ask a Lender
subtitle: The Smart Questions
content:
Ask:

  • What is the interest rate and APR?
  • Is this fixed or ARM? What are the caps?
  • What are total closing costs?
  • Is PMI required? Cost? Removal rules?
  • Will taxes/insurance be escrowed?
  • Is there a prepayment penalty?

title: Wrap-Up
subtitle: Mortgages = Math + Planning
content:
Recap

  • Mortgage payment is more than just the loan
  • Down payment affects LTV, PMI, and total cost
  • Amortization explains the interest-heavy early years
  • Fixed = stable; ARM = risk/reward
If you can understand these basics, you can avoid expensive mistakes.
